title: SEGMENT 3 — Hyperacute rejection: the immune system's instant-kill response

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Hyperacute rejection: the immune system's instant-kill response
I want you to picture, for a minute, what happens if you do the naive experiment.
Take an unmodified pig — a regular pig, the kind your bacon came from — and surgically transplant its kidney into a human.
You sew the artery, you sew the vein, you sew the ureter.
You unclamp the artery.
Blood from the human rushes into the pig kidney.
And the kidney turns black.
Within minutes.
Not hours, not days.
Minutes.
The kidney swells, the vessels burst, the tissue dies, the urine that should be coming out the ureter is replaced by blood.
By the time the surgeon has finished closing the abdomen, the kidney is already a dead, hemorrhagic lump.
That phenomenon has a name.
It is called hyperacute rejection.
And it is the central villain of the last fifty years of xenotransplantation.
Every story I tell you from here on is going to come back to it.
So I want to make sure you understand mechanistically what's happening.
Here is the analogy.
Hyperacute rejection is the immune system's instant-kill — your antibodies see a sugar on the pig organ, complement activates, the organ dies in minutes.
That's the elevator pitch.
Now let me unpack it.
Your blood is not just red cells in salt water.
Your blood is a soup of millions of different antibodies, manufactured by your B cells over the course of your life.
Most of them target specific pathogens you've encountered — flu, rhinovirus, chickenpox.
But some of them target things you've never been deliberately vaccinated against.
They're called natural antibodies, or sometimes pre-formed antibodies.
They're floating in your blood right now, and they bind specifically to sugar structures that human cells don't make but that bacteria, fungi, and many animals do make.
The single most important of these — the chief antigen for hyperacute rejection — is called alpha-gal.
Its full name is galactose-alpha-1,3-galactose.
It is a sugar — specifically, a disaccharide, two sugar molecules linked together in a specific configuration.
Pig cells decorate their surface proteins with alpha-gal all over the place.
So do most mammals — cows, dogs, mice, baboons.
There is one major exception.
The great apes — chimpanzees, gorillas, orangutans — and Old World monkeys, and humans, do not make alpha-gal.
We lost the gene for the enzyme that makes it, roughly twenty-five million years ago, in a common ancestor.
So picture the immune situation.
You, a human, grew up in a world full of bacteria and protozoa that paint themselves with alpha-gal.
Your immune system, very early in life, looks at alpha-gal and says: that's not us.
That's foreign.
Let's make antibodies.
By the time you're a few months old, you have an enormous population of B cells producing anti-alpha-gal antibodies.
They circulate in your blood for your entire life.
Roughly one percent of your total antibody pool is anti-alpha-gal.
One percent.
That's an enormous fraction.
Now you transplant a pig kidney into that person.
The pig kidney is covered in alpha-gal — every glycoprotein on every endothelial cell of every blood vessel inside that kidney has alpha-gal sticking out.
The moment the human's blood enters those vessels, the anti-alpha-gal antibodies in the blood see alpha-gal on the kidney's endothelium and bind.
Tens of thousands of antibodies per cell, instantly.
What happens next is called complement activation.
Complement is a cascade of proteins, also floating around in your blood, that gets switched on whenever antibodies cluster on a surface.

Picture a row of dominoes: an antibody binds, the first complement protein binds the antibody, that protein activates the next, and the next, and the next, until at the end of the cascade you have a structure called the membrane attack complex — a literal molecular drill that punches a hole through the cell membrane.
The endothelial cells lining the pig's blood vessels start dying.
The vessel walls leak.
Platelets activate, clots form, more vessels block, the tissue downstream dies of starvation.
In a matter of minutes, the entire organ is a coagulated, dead lump.
This is hyperacute rejection.
It is not the slower rejection you've heard about with kidney transplants from one human to another — that's a T cell process that takes weeks or months, and we have drugs for it.
Hyperacute rejection is the antibody and complement system, working in seconds to minutes, on a target it has been primed to attack since infancy.
There is no drug that prevents it once it starts.
You cannot dial down preformed antibodies.
You cannot stop the complement cascade fast enough.
So for fifty years, this was the wall.

You could try to remove the antibodies from the patient's blood by filtering — a procedure called plasmapheresis — but you'd remove them for a few hours and then the body would make more, and the new ones would attack the graft.
You could try to inhibit complement with drugs — and several were tried — but you couldn't shut it off completely.
The fundamental problem was that the organ itself was the wrong organ.
It was painted with a sugar that the human body was permanently primed to hate.
The solution, when it finally came, was conceptually simple.
Don't try to suppress the human's response.
Change the pig.
Take the alpha-gal off the pig.
If the pig doesn't make alpha-gal, the antibodies have nothing to bind.
The complement cascade never fires.
Hyperacute rejection doesn't happen.
Saying that and doing it were two different things, of course.
The enzyme that makes alpha-gal is called alpha-1,3-galactosyltransferase, and the gene for it is called GGTA1 — though in xeno papers you usually see it abbreviated as GalT.
To take alpha-gal off a pig, you have to knock out the GalT gene in every cell of the pig's body.
Which, in 2002, when this was finally accomplished, was an extraordinary achievement of genetic engineering.
That's the next segment.
